act-andrew-barr-kurrajong.summary: Stated position and record: Barr is Australia's first openly gay head of a state or territory government, was a prominent advocate for the 'Yes' campaign in the 2017 same-sex marriage postal survey, and his government passed the 2020 legislation banning LGBTI conversion practices.

sources.title: Andrew Barr

act-andrew-braddock-yerrabi.summary: Recorded vote: as an ACT Greens member of the 10th Assembly, Braddock was among the Labor and Greens members who passed the 2024 reforms to the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Act (12 to 5), giving greater choice of gender descriptors on birth certificates, removing the clinical-treatment requirement and lowering the application age, which the Liberals opposed.

sources.title: ACT reforms will make big difference to gender diverse people

act-chris-steel-murrumbidgee.summary: Recorded vote: as a Labor member present in the Assembly in August 2020, Steel was among the Labor and Greens members who voted to pass the Sexuality and Gender Identity Conversion Practices Act 2020 (passed 14 to 11), which the Liberals opposed. Steel also spoke in support of the bill during the debate.

sources.title: LGBT rights in the Australian Capital Territory
ACT Hansard, 27 August 2020 - Sexuality and Gender Identity Conversion Practices Bill 2020

act-ed-cocks-murrumbidgee.summary: Recorded vote: as a Liberal member of the 10th Assembly (elected at a 2022 countback), Cocks was part of the Canberra Liberals who opposed the 2024 reforms to the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Act (which passed 12 to 5), the party objecting in particular to lowering the age at which a person can apply to change their registered sex.

sources.title: ACT reforms will make big difference to gender diverse people

act-elizabeth-lee-kurrajong.summary: Stated position and recorded vote: Lee is described as a moderate who supports gay marriage (Canberra Times); as a Liberal member present in August 2020 she was among the Liberals who opposed the Sexuality and Gender Identity Conversion Practices Act 2020 (passed 14 to 11).

sources.title: Can Elizabeth Lee end the ACT Liberals' 23-year drought?

act-james-milligan-yerrabi.summary: Recorded vote: Milligan served in the Assembly from 2016 to October 2020 and was among the Liberals who opposed the Sexuality and Gender Identity Conversion Practices Act 2020 (passed 14 to 11 in August 2020); he was re-elected in 2024.

sources.title: LGBT rights in the Australian Capital Territory

act-jeremy-hanson-murrumbidgee.summary: Stated position and recorded vote: Hanson has publicly stated his support for same-sex marriage (Canberra Times, 4 June 2015); as a Liberal member present in August 2020 he was among the Liberals who opposed the Sexuality and Gender Identity Conversion Practices Act 2020 (passed 14 to 11).

act-tara-cheyne-ginninderra.summary: Stated position and record: as Human Rights Minister, Cheyne championed the 2024 ACT reforms giving greater choice of gender descriptors on birth certificates, removing the clinical-treatment requirement and lowering the application age, saying the amendments were 'about a person's fundamental human rights, their identity and being able to express who they are'; she also voted with Labor to pass the 2020 conversion practices ban.

LGBT rights in the Australian Capital Territory

act-yvette-berry-ginninderra.summary: Recorded vote: as a Labor member present in the Assembly in August 2020, Berry was among the Labor and Greens members who voted to pass the Sexuality and Gender Identity Conversion Practices Act 2020 (passed 14 to 11), which the Liberals opposed.

sources.title: LGBT rights in the Australian Capital Territory

new-south-wales-alex-greenwich-sydney.summary: Independent MP for Sydney and an openly gay member of the NSW Legislative Assembly (married to Victor Hoeld). A leading LGBTIQ law-reform advocate: former co-chair of Australian Marriage Equality and a key leader of the 2017 'Yes' marriage equality campaign. He introduced and championed the Equality Legislation Amendment (LGBTIQA+) Bill 2023, which passed the Legislative Assembly 50 votes to 30 (16 Oct 2024) and the Legislative Council 15-12 (17 Oct 2024) in amended form, removing barriers to gender recognition (allowing trans people to change birth certificates without surgery). He described the decision to strip back parts of the bill as 'heartbreaking.' In Sept 2024 the Federal Court found a tweet by Mark Latham about Greenwich was defamatory and homophobic, awarding him $140,000.

sources.title: Out Gay Sydney MP Alex Greenwich Reflects On A Decade In NSW Parliament
NSW Passes Equality Bill, Granting Landmark Changes For The Trans Community
Reduced NSW equality bill passes, discrimination remains

new-south-wales-damien-tudehope-statewid.summary: Liberal MLC who criticised the process around the Conversion Practices Ban Bill 2024, with the all-night sitting timing described as an 'abuse of democracy.' He was among Coalition members who sought amendments during the debate, including provisions around religious freedom.

sources.title: Conversion therapy bill passes NSW parliament after marathon debate

new-south-wales-felicity-wilson-north-sh.summary: Liberal MP for North Shore. She crossed the floor to support the Equality Legislation Amendment (LGBTIQA+) Bill 2024, defying Liberal party lines (the party did not grant a conscience vote). She said: 'As a Liberal I believe in the fundamental principle of equality for all people, regardless of their gender identity or sexual orientation,' and 'Just because the party won't give you a conscience vote, doesn't mean that you don't have a conscience.' She argued the bill 'will not harm any person in NSW' and called claims it would create risk for women 'fearmongering.' She had previously supported the 'Yes' vote for marriage equality.

sources.title: Felicity Wilson defies party
NSW Passes Equality Bill, Granting Landmark Changes For The Trans Community

new-south-wales-greg-donnelly-statewide.summary: Labor MLC (since 2005), a Catholic who has been a consistent opponent of LGBTQ-affirming legislation. He opposed a NSW Greens marriage equality bill (2012); in May 2017 was one of three Labor MPs to vote with the Coalition to block decriminalisation of abortion; wrote against same-sex adoption in NSW; and wrote an opinion piece opposing the Safe Schools program (Nov 2016). He publicly urged people to sign a petition calling on the Legislative Council to vote against Alex Greenwich's Equality Bill, arguing the reforms risked 'far-reaching unintended consequences for women and children' and would let 'males legally identify as females' and access women-only spaces. In June 2023 he spoke at a 'Why Can't Women Talk About Sex' event alongside Moira Deeming and Tania Mihailuk.

sources.title: Greg Donnelly

new-south-wales-jacqui-munro-statewide.summary: Liberal MLC who spoke in support of the Equality Legislation Amendment (LGBTIQA+) Bill 2024 (although absent from the final vote). She said: 'I believe that gender fluidity and expression without medicalisation or formalised labels can and should be accepted.'

sources.title: NSW Passes Equality Bill, Granting Landmark Changes For The Trans Community

new-south-wales-jenny-leong-newtown.summary: Greens MP for Newtown who supported LGBTIQ equality but criticised the scaled-back Equality Legislation Amendment (LGBTIQA+) Bill 2024, saying the reduced bill 'leaves the queer community vulnerable.'

sources.title: Reduced NSW equality bill passes, discrimination remains

new-south-wales-john-ruddick-statewide.summary: Libertarian Party MLC, the first NSW Libertarian elected to Parliament (April 2023). During the Conversion Practices Ban Bill 2024 debate he moved an amendment to remove 'gender identity' from the bill, prioritising individual autonomy in choosing counselling. He said: 'We support individuals or institutions offering a counselling service that attempts to convince people that they are not same-sex attracted or that they are not transgender,' while adding he did not support practices involving 'physical assault or mental anguish' as those are already crimes. The amendment was defeated.

sources.title: John Ruddick
Conversion therapy bill passes NSW parliament after marathon debate

new-south-wales-mark-latham-statewide.summary: One Nation MLC (sat as independent for part of the term) known for vocal opposition to transgender and LGBTIQ measures. He introduced the Education Legislation Amendment (Parental Rights) Bill, which sought to prohibit teaching of 'gender fluidity' in schools, restrict trans students' use of facilities and sports, and require school staff to disclose a student's gender identity to parents; the NSW Government rejected it. During the Conversion Practices Ban Bill 2024 debate he opposed the late-night sitting, calling it a 'shameful tactic' and the 'politics of exhaustion,' and moved a motion to delay the debate. In Sept 2024 the Federal Court ruled a tweet he posted about Alex Greenwich was defamatory and homophobic.

sources.title: NSW Government Rejects One Nation MLC Mark Latham's Anti-Trans Education Bill
Alex Greenwich
Conversion therapy bill passes NSW parliament after marathon debate

new-south-wales-penny-sharpe-statewide.summary: Labor MLC and Leader of the Government in the Legislative Council. She was the first openly lesbian member of the NSW Parliament (appointed 2005) and is a long-standing LGBTIQ rights advocate. She worked internally within Labor to shift the party toward supporting marriage equality and campaigned during the 2017 Marriage Law Postal Survey. In 2018 she championed cross-party amendments to the Public Health Act to establish abortion-clinic 'safe access zones.' During the Equality Bill debate she defended the legislation, stating it 'does not impact on women's rights.'

sources.title: Penny Sharpe
NSW Passes Equality Bill, Granting Landmark Changes For The Trans Community

new-south-wales-sarah-mitchell-statewide.summary: Nationals MLC who, as NSW Education Minister (Perrottet Government), delivered the government's response rejecting Mark Latham's Education Legislation Amendment (Parental Rights) Bill (announced March 2022). She warned the bill had 'the potential to marginalise the LGBTIQ+ community, which is a group already experiencing increased isolation and vulnerability,' and said 'the NSW Government supports the right of every student and teacher to be themselves at school.'

sources.title: NSW Government reject Mark Latham's anti-trans education bill

new-south-wales-susan-carter-statewide.summary: Liberal MLC who argued against the Equality Legislation Amendment (LGBTIQA+) Bill 2024, citing concerns about single-sex spaces. She was also among the members who moved or supported amendments during the Conversion Practices Ban Bill 2024 debate.

sources.title: NSW Passes Equality Bill, Granting Landmark Changes For The Trans Community

new-south-wales-tamara-smith-ballina.summary: Greens MP for Ballina and party education spokesperson who supported LGBTIQ equality but criticised the scaled-back Equality Bill, calling the removed private-school anti-discrimination protections 'the most significant piece of reform' that was left 'missing in action.'

sources.title: Reduced NSW equality bill passes, discrimination remains

nt-chansey-paech-gwoja.summary: In his maiden speech to the NT Legislative Assembly on 19 October 2016, Paech said "I am young, I am gay, I am black," called for same-sex marriage recognition ("I look forward to the day when this country will recognise my rights as equal rights, when I too can marry in my country"), and stated "I will never allow or tolerate discrimination, homophobia or hate speech in this Assembly." This is a stated position; Paech is the first openly gay Indigenous MP in Australia.

sources.title: Full Text: Chansey Paech's maiden speech to parliament
Chansey Paech

nt-justine-davis-johnston.summary: In October 2025, independent MLA Justine Davis opposed the government's changes affecting transgender people in prisons, stating in an SBS interview "There is no need for these changes and, in fact, they are going to potentially cause harm." This is a stated position.

sources.title: 'Degraded and uneasy': Incarcerated trans women push back on NT prison ban proposal

nt-lia-finocchiaro-spillett.summary: As Chief Minister, Finocchiaro announced in October 2025 a policy to house NT prisoners according to sex assigned at birth, removing transgender women from women's prisons, stating "If you're born a bloke, you're going to a men's prison" and framing it as being about "women's safety" and "women's dignity"; she said the policy reversed the former Labor government's approach allowing self-declaration. This is a stated position and announced government policy.

sources.title: NT Govt Bans Trans Women From Women's Prisons in “Nation-First” Move
'Degraded and uneasy': Incarcerated trans women push back on NT prison ban proposal

nt-robyn-lambley-araluen.summary: During the August 2024 NT election campaign, the Australian Christian Lobby recorded that independent MLA Robyn Lambley (along with all CLP candidates) supported a review into the medical and surgical care of gender-diverse children; as Speaker she subsequently tabled the ACL's petition (17-18 October 2024) calling for the suspension of gender-affirming treatments for children in the NT. Tabling a petition is a procedural duty; her support for a review is the recorded stated position.

sources.title: MPs urged to suspend gender-affirming care for kids

nt-selena-uibo-arnhem.summary: As Opposition Leader during parliamentary question time in October 2025, Uibo challenged the government's transgender prisoner policy, telling the Chief Minister that despite the announced ban, "internal department documents state that you have not, and you still operate your government under the 2021 version four directive." This is a stated position recorded in parliament.

sources.title: 'Degraded and uneasy': Incarcerated trans women push back on NT prison ban proposal

queensland-amanda-stoker-oodgeroo.summary: Has publicly opposed aspects of transgender rights, advocating restricting transgender participation in women's sport and limiting access to gender-affirming care for children; in 2018 opposed removing religious-school exemptions relating to sexual orientation and gender identity, and has described sexuality as a 'choice' (stated positions).

sources.title: Amanda Stoker

queensland-david-crisafulli-broadwater.summary: Premier whose government implemented the 2025 pause on initiating puberty blockers and gender-affirming hormones for new under-18 public patients (stated/enacted government position). His Wikipedia profile records that he opposes transgender women competing in women's sport and voted for an unsuccessful bill to ban it.

sources.title: David Crisafulli
The Ongoing Fight Against Queensland's Trans Youth Healthcare Ban

queensland-jarrod-bleijie-kawana.summary: Mixed record: as Attorney-General in 2012 introduced legislation removing state-sanctioned ceremonies from same-sex civil partnerships and legislation barring single people and same-sex couples from surrogacy; in 2015 he declared support for same-sex marriage ahead of the federal plebiscite (stated positions / legislative actions).

sources.title: Jarrod Bleijie

queensland-michael-berkman-maiwar.summary: Greens MP who publicly opposes the 2025 Queensland ban/pause on puberty blockers and hormone therapy for trans minors, stating 'Hormone therapy and puberty blockers save lives' and that the treatment is lifesaving for young people with gender dysphoria (stated position).

sources.title: Hormone Therapy Ban: Interview with Greens MP Michael Berkman
Doctors, activists condemn Queensland LNP's attack on trans youth

queensland-robbie-katter-traeger.summary: In 2022 moved to legislate banning transgender athletes from women's sport; his motion on the issue was defeated 49-33, with the LNP opposition voting in favour (stated position / recorded vote on his motion).

sources.title: Robbie Katter

queensland-shannon-fentiman-waterford.summary: Recorded as supporting transgender rights and opposed to the gender-critical movement; as Child Safety Minister championed 2016 legislation allowing same-sex couples to adopt (stated position). (As Attorney-General she also introduced the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Bill in late 2022, though this specific detail was not on the fetched source.)

sources.title: Shannon Fentiman

queensland-tim-nicholls-clayfield.summary: As Health Minister, in January 2025 announced an immediate pause on prescribing puberty blockers (Stage 1) and cross-sex hormones (Stage 2) to new under-18 patients in Queensland public health, citing 'contested evidence'; after a court quashed the directive in October 2025 he reissued it the same day (stated position / ministerial action).

sources.title: Tim Nicholls
The Ongoing Fight Against Queensland's Trans Youth Healthcare Ban

south-australia-cory-bernardi-statewide.summary: One Nation MLC and party state leader; former Liberal senator. A long-standing opponent of same-sex marriage: in a federal speech he linked redefining marriage to bestiality, and in 2026 said he stands by those comments '100 per cent.' In his SA maiden speech to the Legislative Council on 20 May 2026 he criticised gender-affirming healthcare, stating 'the government allows doctors to mutilate children in the name of gender-affirming care,' prompting Greens MLCs to walk out.

sources.title: Cory Bernardi Backs Gay Marriage And Bestiality Claims Ahead Of Election
Cory Bernardi's Maiden Speech To SA Parliament Sparks Walkout

south-australia-ian-hunter-statewide.summary: Long-serving Labor MLC and the first openly gay/LGBTI member of the SA Parliament and Cabinet. A prominent same-sex marriage advocate: delivered a 2009 parliamentary speech calling for marriage equality and co-sponsored a 2011 Marriage Equality Bill with Greens MLC Tammy Franks, at one point calling Labor's then-policy on marriage 'morally bankrupt'. He married his partner overseas before Australian law recognised same-sex marriage. In Nov 2023 he spoke in the Legislative Council relaying a survivor's account in support of banning conversion practices.

sources.title: Ian Hunter (politician)
Conversion practices

south-australia-jason-virgo-mackillop.summary: One Nation MP for MacKillop, elected April 2026. Earlier in life he was an LGBT and marriage-equality activist: he organised marriage-equality and IDAHO (International Day Against Homophobia) rallies in Adelaide around 2011, was involved with gay and lesbian counselling services, and ran for the Australian Sex Party in 2010. Asked in 2026 about his political evolution he said 'we grow up, we learn new things, we change our views as we age,' and declined to elaborate on his current positions on LGBT rights.

sources.title: Gay rights activist now frontline SA One Nation pollie

south-australia-kyam-maher-statewide.summary: Labor MLC and Attorney-General. Drafted the Conversion Practices Prohibition Bill 2024, the Malinauskas Government's legislation banning LGBTIQ conversion practices (passed October 2024, effective 1 April 2025). Publicly described conversion practices as 'a damaging practice that seeks to force members of the LGBTIQ community to abandon who they are under the guise of "saving" or "helping" them.'

sources.title: Conversion practices to be banned
Conversion practices

south-australia-laura-henderson-statewid.summary: Liberal MLC. In the Legislative Council on 24 September 2024 she spoke against the Conversion Practices Prohibition Bill 2024 (a conscience vote for the Liberal Party), calling it 'incredible overreach into the personal lives of people in this state' and arguing 'it is not for us as a parliament to tell parents what conversations they can and cannot have.' She raised concerns about the bill's definitions and parental rights.

sources.title: Conversion Practices Prohibition Bill

south-australia-melanie-selwood-statewid.summary: Greens MLC elected at the March 2026 state election. On 20 May 2026 she walked out of the Legislative Council, alongside fellow Greens MLC Robert Simms, during One Nation MLC Cory Bernardi's maiden speech in protest at his comments about transgender people and gender-affirming healthcare. She has aligned with the Greens' platform of advancing LGBTIQ+ rights and accessible gender-affirming healthcare.

sources.title: Cory Bernardi's Maiden Speech To SA Parliament Sparks Walkout

south-australia-nat-cook-hurtle-vale.summary: Labor MP for Hurtle Vale (elected Speaker of the House of Assembly in May 2026, sitting as independent in that role). As Shadow Human Services Minister she drafted private legislation to outlaw LGBTIQ conversion therapy in SA (reported July 2020), proposing penalties of up to eight years' imprisonment, an early step toward what became the Conversion Practices Prohibition Act 2024. As Human Services Minister she engaged extensively with LGBTIQ policy, including implementation of the 2024 conversion practices ban.

sources.title: SA move to outlaw conversion therapy

south-australia-robert-simms-statewide.summary: Greens MLC and SA Greens leader; openly gay and the first out gay man to represent SA in federal parliament. Long-standing LGBTIQ advocate: as a federal senator was Greens spokesperson on marriage equality; in SA has introduced birth-certificate reform for rainbow families and a bill to remove religious exemptions from anti-discrimination laws, and supported the 2024 conversion practices ban (moving amendments with Tammy Franks). On 20 May 2026 he walked out of the Legislative Council during One Nation MLC Cory Bernardi's maiden speech over Bernardi's remarks on transgender people and gender-affirming care.

sources.title: SA Greens announce plans to advance the rights of LGBTIQ+ people
Cory Bernardi's Maiden Speech To SA Parliament Sparks Walkout

south-australia-sarah-game-statewide.summary: Legislative Council member (elected for One Nation in 2022; in 2026 announced she would join the Family First Party). Per her Wikipedia profile she has stated she 'does not hold to strict interpretations of complex personal issues relating to abortion and transgender issues,' indicating a non-dogmatic stance; no detailed voting record on LGBTQ/gender bills is documented in the sourced material reviewed.

sources.title: Sarah Game

tasmania-bridget-archer-bass.summary: In February 2022, as federal MP, Archer crossed the floor over the religious discrimination bill; she said she was "horrified" that proposed protections would not extend "to children who identify as transgender" and "utterly distressed by this exclusion," and said she could not support the bill in that form. (Stated position/floor-crossing.)

sources.title: 'What message are we sending?' Archer slams government bill

tasmania-cassy-o-connor-hobart.summary: As Greens leader O'Connor championed the 2019 gender-optional birth certificate reforms, saying "Just as it was with the vote on marriage equality, the sky is likely to remain in place with the passing of this legislation"; in a February 2023 parliamentary speech she supported a ban on conversion practices, stating "The Greens choose love, acceptance and inclusion" and speaking as the mother of a transgender son. (Stated positions/parliamentary speech.)

sources.title: Tasmania passes landmark laws for gender-optional birth certificates
Legislation to Prohibit Conversion Practices

tasmania-david-o-byrne-franklin.summary: In Equality Tasmania's 2025 state election material O'Byrne is recorded as a strong ally supporting marriage equality, conversion-therapy bans, intersex protections, improved access to gender-affirming care, and hate-crime protections. (Stated positions.)

sources.title: Tasmanian State Election 2025

tasmania-dean-winter-franklin.summary: OUTInPerth describes Winter as "an early advocate for marriage equality." (Reported characterisation.)

sources.title: Peter Gutwein's Liberal government returned in Tasmanian election

tasmania-ella-haddad-clark.summary: As Labor's then shadow attorney-general, Haddad opposed the federal Religious Freedom draft bills, saying the changes "would unnecessarily risk hard-won anti-discrimination rights and, if passed, would expose vulnerable Australians to increased discrimination with no recourse." (Stated position.)

sources.title: Follow Tasmania's lead on religious freedom laws says Labor's Ella Haddad

tasmania-eric-abetz-franklin.summary: In a November 2017 Senate speech Abetz argued that "same-sex marriage is not a human right" in the way religious freedoms and heterosexual marriage are, and opposed the inclusion of trans, intersex and non-binary protections in the marriage legislation. (Stated position.)

sources.title: Eric Abetz says "same-sex marriage is not a human right" in wild Senate speech

tasmania-felix-ellis-braddon.summary: At a July 2022 public meeting on the Tasmanian Law Reform Institute's conversion-practices report, Ellis noted there would be "a formal consultation period that all Tasmanians can take part in before any legislation is tabled," said he wanted to hear from constituents and would "listen with open ears and an open heart." (Stated position.)

sources.title: Standing up for traditional biblical views on sexuality and gender in Tasmania

tasmania-guy-barnett-lyons.summary: Wikipedia records that Barnett opposed same-sex marriage during the 2017 postal survey campaign, including a September 2017 claim that "'Gay sex' will be taught in school if SSM vote passes." (Stated position.)

sources.title: Guy Barnett (Australian politician)

tasmania-jeremy-rockliff-braddon.summary: Rockliff voted for the Greens-sponsored 2015 motion supporting same-sex marriage (which passed the House 15-9), stating he was "absolutely unequivocally in favour of same-sex marriage equality"; as Premier in 2022 he committed to banning conversion practices, saying "I will be leading the change" and that change was needed given the harm to wellbeing. (Recorded vote and stated positions.)

sources.title: New Tasmanian Premier Jeremy Rockliff Had Voted For Pro-LGBT Laws
Premier Jeremy Rockliff Says Tasmania Will Ban 'Conversion Therapy'

tasmania-kerry-vincent-prosser.summary: In the 2024 Equality Tasmania Legislative Council election survey Vincent (Liberal) referenced the government's existing 2023 conversion-practices bill and said work on intersex issues should be done collaboratively "with community and clinical stakeholders"; he answered "No" on a Human Rights Act and gave a neutral response on drag story time. (Stated positions.)

sources.title: Tasmanian Legislative Council Election 2024

tasmania-kristie-johnston-clark.summary: In the 2024 Equality Tasmania election survey Johnston said she wants to end conversion practices ("Absolutely"), would enact a prohibition as "Very high priority. It is long overdue," supported prohibiting medically-unnecessary interventions on intersex children, and answered "Yes" to all the survey's major LGBTIQA+ reform questions. (Stated positions.)

sources.title: Response from Kristie Johnston, Independent, Clark

tasmania-michael-ferguson-bass.summary: Wikipedia records that Ferguson opposed same-sex marriage and that in 2018 he criticised the Tasmanian Greens and Labor for their reforms making birth certificates gender-optional. (Stated positions.)

sources.title: Michael Ferguson (Australian politician)

tasmania-mike-gaffney-mersey.summary: During the November 2018 Legislative Council debate on the gender/birth-certificate reform bill, Gaffney said he could not support certain provisions without sufficient information, stating his job was "for good legislation and to review what's in front of us" and warning that passing flawed legislation would "put your cause back even further." (Stated position during debate.)

sources.title: Birth certificate changes delayed in Tasmanian Parliament

tasmania-roger-jaensch-braddon.summary: OUTInPerth describes Jaensch as having "been a supporter of LGBTIQ+ equality for many years and a voice for LGBTIQ+ law reform in the Liberal Party." (Reported characterisation of his record.)

sources.title: Peter Gutwein's Liberal government returned in Tasmanian election

tasmania-rosalie-woodruff-franklin.summary: As Greens leader Woodruff said the party would "move that Parliament ban conversion practices and fully implement" the Law Reform Institute recommendations, and criticised the Liberal government's draft bill as one that "would legitimise conversion practices" and increase "trauma, pain and suffering of LGBTIQA+ people"; in the 2024 Equality Tasmania survey the Greens (Woodruff responding) backed a real conversion-practices ban, opposed weakening gender-recognition laws, and supported evidence-based care for trans and gender-diverse youth. (Stated positions.)

sources.title: Tasmanian Greens pledge ban on conversion practices
Response from the Tasmanian Greens

tasmania-rosemary-armitage-launceston.summary: During the November 2018 Legislative Council debate on the gender/birth-certificate reform bill, Armitage stated she could not support the bill as presented. (Stated position during debate.)

sources.title: Birth certificate changes delayed in Tasmanian Parliament

tasmania-tania-rattray-mcintyre.summary: During the November 2018 Legislative Council debate on the gender/birth-certificate reform bill, Rattray stated she could not support the bill as presented. (Stated position during debate.)

sources.title: Birth certificate changes delayed in Tasmanian Parliament

tasmania-vica-bayley-clark.summary: In December 2023 parliamentary estimates Bayley criticised the government's 2015 LGBTIQA+ framework for "the complete lack of acknowledgement of Tasmanians who are non-binary and don't identify as male or female," and pressed the government to update the framework with the LGBTIQA+ community. (Stated position/parliamentary statement.)

sources.title: Minister For Health – LGBTIQA+
